name: Cornucopia Extended
description: The Cornucopia Extended is a mythical horn of plenty, overflowing with an inexhaustible supply of whatever its wielder desires. Originating from the myth of Amalthea, the goat-nymph who nurtured the infant Zeus, the original cornucopia was created when Zeus accidentally broke off one of her horns. He then imbued it with the power to provide endless nourishment and abundance. The Cornucopia Extended, however, is a later, more potent iteration of this artifact. Legends say it was forged by Hephaestus, imbued with the combined blessings of Demeter, goddess of agriculture, and Plutus, god of wealth. This version not only provides food and drink, but also grants wishes for material possessions, knowledge, and even abstract concepts like happiness or peace, albeit with a caveat: the wielder must use its bounty for the benefit of others, or the horn will turn to dust. It represents prosperity, gratitude, and the importance of sharing one's blessings. Its appearance varies in different accounts, sometimes described as a goat's horn, other times as a wicker basket overflowing with fruits, vegetables, and flowers. The Cornucopia Extended stands as a symbol of the Earth's bounty and the potential for limitless generosity.
subtitle: Horn of endless, selfless abundance
origin: Forged by Hephaestus, blessed by Demeter and Plutus.

keyMyths.title: The Origin of the Cornucopia
keyMyths.description: Zeus broke off a horn of the goat Amalthea, who nursed him. He then blessed it to always provide whatever its owner desired.

keyMyths.title: The Cornucopia Extended's Blessing
keyMyths.description: Hephaestus created the Cornucopia Extended, and it was blessed by Demeter and Plutus to provide limitless abundance, but only for selfless purposes.
keyMyths.source: Fictional account based on Greek mythology

sources.author: Pseudo-Apollodorus
sources.description: A comprehensive source of Greek myths and legends.
sources.text: A comprehensive source of Greek myths and legends.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: An account of the origins of the gods and the cosmos.
